section.title: Before the Sanhedrin and Peter's Denial
section.sectionRationale: Jesus stands before the high priest and the Sanhedrin, silent against false witnesses until He is put under oath, when He confesses His identity as the Christ and the coming Son of Man, and is condemned for blasphemy. Outside, in the same hour, Peter denies three times that he even knows Him.
section.thesis: Under oath before His judges, Jesus confesses the truth that condemns Him, that He is the Christ, the Son of God, and the coming Son of Man, while Peter, in the courtyard below, denies Him three times; the faithful witness and the failing disciple are set side by side.
section.frameNote: Two parallel beats: Jesus' trial before the Sanhedrin, His confession, and His condemnation for blasphemy; and Peter's threefold denial in the courtyard, ending in bitter tears.

units.label: Before the Sanhedrin
units.note: the good confession and the charge of blasphemy

verses.text: Those who had arrested Jesus led Him away to the house of Caiaphas the high priest, where the scribes and elders had gathered.
But Peter followed Him at a distance, right up to the courtyard of the high priest. And he went in and sat down with the guards to see the outcome.
Now the chief priests and the whole Sanhedrin were seeking false testimony against Jesus in order to put Him to death.
But they did not find any, though many false witnesses came forward. Finally two came forward
and declared, “This man said, ‘I am able to destroy the temple of God and rebuild it in three days.’”
So the high priest stood up and asked Him, “Have You no answer? What are these men testifying against You?”
But Jesus remained silent. Then the high priest said to Him, “I charge You under oath by the living God: Tell us if You are the {{christos|Christ}}, the Son of God.”
“You have said it yourself,” Jesus answered. “But I say to all of you, from now on you will see the Son of Man sitting at the right hand of Power and coming on the clouds of heaven.”
At this, the high priest tore his clothes and declared, “He has {{blasphemeo|blasphemed}}! Why do we need any more witnesses? Look, now you have heard the blasphemy.
What do you think?” “He deserves to die,” they answered.
Then they spit in His face and struck Him. Others slapped Him
and said, “Prophesy to us, Christ! Who hit You?”
Meanwhile, Peter was sitting out in the courtyard, and a servant girl came up to him. “You also were with Jesus the Galilean,” she said.
But he {{arneomai|denied}} it before them all: “I do not know what you are talking about.”
When Peter had gone out to the gateway, another servant girl saw him and said to the people there, “This man was with Jesus of Nazareth.”
And again he denied it with an oath: “I do not know the man!”
After a little while, those standing nearby came up to Peter. “Surely you are one of them,” they said, “for your accent gives you away.”
At that he began to curse and swear to them, “I do not know the man!” And immediately a rooster crowed.
Then Peter remembered the word that Jesus had spoken: “Before the rooster crows, you will deny Me three times.” And he went outside and wept bitterly.

christos.gloss: The Anointed One, the Messiah. Put under oath, Jesus does not deny that He is the Christ, the Son of God; His confession before the court is the truth that seals His condemnation and the truth on which the church stands.

blasphemeo.gloss: To blaspheme, to speak impiously against God. The high priest judges Jesus' claim to be the enthroned, coming Son of Man to be blasphemy; the terrible irony is that the charge would be true only if the claim were false, but it is true, and the real blasphemy is theirs.

arneomai.gloss: To deny, disown, refuse to acknowledge. Three times Peter denies even knowing Jesus, the very failure Jesus foretold; the word is the dark counterpart to confession, and Peter's bitter tears open the way to his later restoration.

basic.context: Jesus is taken to Caiaphas, where the council seeks testimony to condemn Him but finds only false, conflicting witnesses. Jesus stays silent until the high priest puts Him under oath to say whether He is the Christ, the Son of God. He answers that He is, and that they will see the Son of Man enthroned at God's right hand and coming on the clouds. The high priest tears his robes and calls it blasphemy; they condemn Him to death and abuse Him. Meanwhile, in the courtyard, Peter three times denies knowing Jesus, then hears the rooster and weeps bitterly.
basic.hermeneutics: Matthew tells the trial and the denial together on purpose: the faithful confession of Jesus inside contrasts with the failing denial of Peter outside. Jesus' answer joins Psalm 110 and Daniel 7, claiming divine enthronement, which is why the priest cries blasphemy. Read Peter's fall not as the end but, with his tears, as the beginning of grace.
basic.originalLanguages: Under oath, Jesus confesses that He is the Christos, the Messiah and Son of God. The high priest calls it blasphemeo, blasphemy. And Peter, three times, does the opposite of confession: he arneomai, denies, that he knows Jesus at all.
basic.typology: Jesus is the silent, suffering servant of Isaiah 53 who does not open His mouth against false accusation. His claim fulfills the Son of Man of Daniel 7 and the enthroned Lord of Psalm 110. The false temple charge points to the true temple, His body.
basic.christ: Jesus is the faithful witness who makes the good confession before His judges, the Son of God and the coming Son of Man, who accepts condemnation and abuse in silence, drinking the cup for the very people who condemn Him, including a denying Peter.
basic.god: God's purpose stands even in a rigged trial; the truth of who Jesus is shines out under oath. God is the living God by whom the priest swears, and He will vindicate His Son by seating Him at His right hand.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 53 gives the silent, condemned servant. Daniel 7 gives the Son of Man on the clouds. Psalm 110 gives the Lord at God's right hand. Luke 22 records Jesus' look that met Peter's denial.
basic.oneStory: Inside, the true King confesses His identity and is condemned for telling the truth; outside, His chief disciple denies he ever knew Him. The faithful witness and the failing follower are held together, and the gospel is in that contrast: Jesus is condemned in the place of deniers like Peter, and like us. His bitter tears are not the last word, for the One being condemned will rise and restore him, and the story moves on toward the cross where the good confession is sealed in blood.

context.literary: Matthew interleaves the trial and the denial, framing Jesus' faithful confession with Peter's failure by placing Peter in the courtyard in verse 58 and returning to him in verse 69. The false temple charge recalls Jesus' cleansing of the temple and points forward to the mockery at the cross. The scene fulfills Jesus' predictions of both His suffering and Peter's denial in the same night.
context.historical: The Sanhedrin was the Jewish ruling council; a night trial with a hastily assembled quorum and a rush to a capital verdict violated their own norms, marking the proceedings as unjust. Two agreeing witnesses were required by law. Tearing one's clothes was the prescribed response to hearing blasphemy. A Galilean accent was distinct in Jerusalem, exposing Peter. Roosters crowing marked the pre-dawn hours.
context.canonical: Jesus' silence fulfills Isaiah 53:7, the servant who did not open His mouth. His answer combines Daniel 7:13, the Son of Man coming with the clouds, and Psalm 110:1, seated at God's right hand, claiming divine authority. The good confession before authorities is echoed in 1 Timothy 6:13. Peter's fall and weeping set up his threefold restoration in John 21.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To present Jesus as the faithful witness who confesses His true identity under oath and is unjustly condemned, fulfilling Scripture, and to show, in Peter's denial, both the weakness of the flesh and the beginning of grace through repentant tears.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The trial describes Jesus' unique redemptive suffering and confession. Peter's denial describes a real failure that warns and instructs. The passage prescribes, by Jesus' example, faithful confession under pressure, and, by Peter's tears, the path of repentance after failure.

debates.question: Why was Jesus' answer judged to be blasphemy?

views.label: The claim to divine enthronement and coming
views.case: By joining Daniel 7 and Psalm 110, Jesus claimed to sit at God's right hand and come on the clouds, a claim to share God's throne and authority; to the high priest this was a man arrogating divine status, hence the charge of blasphemy.

views.label: The messianic and Son of God claim itself
views.case: The priest's question was whether Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God; His affirmation, from one they deemed a deceiver bound for death, was taken as an intolerable, God-dishonoring claim, so the messianic confession before them was itself judged blasphemous.

debates.bearing: Either way, the leaders rightly saw that Jesus claimed divine prerogatives; their error was not misunderstanding the claim but rejecting it, for the claim is true, and the real dishonor to God was condemning His Son.

study.originalLanguages: Silent against false witnesses, Jesus speaks only when the high priest puts Him under oath by the living God to say whether He is the Christos, the Messiah, the Son of God. He does not deny it, and He goes further, declaring that they will see the Son of Man enthroned at the right hand of Power and coming on the clouds, weaving together Daniel 7 and Psalm 110. The high priest tears his robes and cries blasphemeo, blasphemy, and the council condemns Him. In the courtyard, Peter does the opposite of the good confession: three times he arneomai, denies, that he even knows Jesus, until the rooster crows and, remembering, he weeps bitterly. Confession and denial stand together in the same dark hour.

typology.fulfillment: Jesus who did not open His mouth against His accusers, fulfilling Isaiah 53:7.

typology.type: The Son of Man on the clouds
typology.fulfillment: Daniel 7:13-14, claimed by Jesus and vindicated in His resurrection and return.

typology.type: The Lord at God's right hand
typology.fulfillment: Psalm 110:1, the enthronement Jesus foretells to His judges, fulfilled in His exaltation, Acts 2:33-36.

study.christ: Jesus is the faithful and true witness who, before the highest court of His people, makes the good confession: He is the Christ, the Son of God, and the coming Son of Man enthroned at the right hand of Power. He meets false accusation with dignified silence, fulfilling the servant of Isaiah, and accepts condemnation, spitting, and blows without retaliation. The deepest irony is that the truth of His confession is the reason He is condemned; He is sentenced for being who He is. And He bears this in the place of failing sinners, including the very disciple denying Him in the courtyard, so that His faithful witness covers Peter's, and ours.
study.god: God's saving purpose advances even through an unjust, unlawful trial, for the truth of the Son shines out precisely under oath by the living God. God will vindicate the One His court condemns, seating Him at His right hand and sending Him on the clouds, so that the judges will one day stand before the One they judged. And God is gracious beyond the failure of His people: Peter's denial does not cancel God's plan, and the bitter tears God grants are the seed of repentance and restoration, showing a God who redeems even His disciples' worst hours.

crossReferences.note: Oppressed and afflicted, yet He did not open His mouth, fulfilled in Jesus' silence.
The Son of Man coming with the clouds, claimed by Jesus before the council.
Sit at My right hand, the enthronement Jesus foretells to His judges.
Peter's threefold restoration answering his threefold denial.

study.oneStory: Inside the high priest's house the true King confesses who He is and is condemned for telling the truth; outside, His chief disciple denies he ever knew Him. The faithful witness and the failing follower are held together, and the gospel lives in that contrast: Jesus is condemned in the place of deniers like Peter, and like us. Peter's bitter tears are not the end, for the One now condemned will rise and restore him. The story presses on to the cross, where the good confession is sealed in blood, and to the resurrection, where the enthroned Son of Man is vindicated.

quiz.q: How did Jesus respond when put under oath about His identity?

options.text: He confessed that He is the Christ, the Son of God, and the coming Son of Man at God's right hand.
options.note: Verse 64 gives Jesus' full confession, joining Daniel 7 and Psalm 110.

options.text: He admitted only that others called Him the Christ.
options.note: He affirmed it as true and added the Son of Man claim, not merely reporting others' words.

options.text: He stayed completely silent throughout the trial.
options.note: He was silent against false witnesses but spoke clearly when put under oath.

options.text: He denied being the Messiah to avoid death.
options.note: He confessed the truth even though it led to His condemnation.

quiz.q: Why did the high priest charge Jesus with blasphemy?

options.text: Because Jesus claimed divine enthronement, to sit at God's right hand and come on the clouds.
options.note: His claim to share God's throne and authority was heard as blasphemy.

options.text: Because Jesus refused to answer the council's questions.
options.note: His silence frustrated them, but the blasphemy charge arose from His confession, not His silence.

options.text: Because the false witnesses proved their case against Him.
options.note: The witnesses failed; the verdict rested on His own words, not their testimony.

options.text: Because He admitted to a crime against Rome.
options.note: The charge was religious blasphemy before the Sanhedrin, not a Roman crime.

quiz.q: What does Matthew emphasize by telling the trial and Peter's denial together?

options.text: The contrast between Jesus' faithful confession inside and Peter's denial outside.
options.note: The interwoven scenes set the faithful witness against the failing disciple.

options.text: That Peter was braver than the other disciples.
options.note: Peter did follow at a distance, but the point is his failure set against Jesus' faithfulness, not his bravery.

options.text: That the trial and the denial happened on different nights.
options.note: They happened the same night, deliberately interwoven; this misreads the timing.

options.text: That Jesus and Peter both denied the truth under pressure.
options.note: Jesus confessed the truth; only Peter denied, which is the whole contrast.

quiz.q: What is significant about Peter's response after the rooster crowed?

options.text: He remembered Jesus' word and wept bitterly, the grief that opens the way to repentance.
options.note: Verse 75 shows Peter's bitter tears, the beginning of genuine repentance.

options.text: He felt regret but quickly moved on.
options.note: His weeping was deep and bitter, not a passing regret; this understates it.

options.text: He immediately returned to defend Jesus at the trial.
options.note: He went outside and wept; he did not return to the trial.

options.text: He decided he was no longer a disciple at all.
options.note: His tears lead toward restoration, not final abandonment of Christ.

questions: Jesus told the saving truth about Himself even though it cost Him His life. Where is faithfulness to the truth about Christ costly for you, and are you willing to pay it?
Peter denied Jesus when a servant girl's question made it risky to be known as a follower. In what small, ordinary settings are you tempted to distance yourself from Christ?
The same night held both a faithful confession and a threefold denial. When you fail Christ, do you run from Him in shame or, like Peter's tears, turn back toward Him?
Jesus was condemned for being exactly who He claimed to be. How does His willingness to be sentenced for the truth deepen your trust in Him?
Peter's bitter weeping was not the end of his story. How does knowing that Christ restores failing disciples free you to repent honestly rather than hide?

translationNotes.note: The charge distorts Jesus' saying about the temple; John 2:19-21 shows He spoke of the temple of His body, so the accusation twists a true saying into a false one.
Jesus combines Daniel 7:13, the Son of Man coming on the clouds, with Psalm 110:1, sitting at the right hand of Power; right hand of Power is a reverent way of saying at God's own right hand.
You have said it yourself is an affirmative answer in that idiom, equivalent to yes, it is as you say, not an evasion.
